title: Compare synthetic probe metrics in Explore

blocks.content:
Grafana **Explore** is where you query metrics time series with PromQL. In Grafana Cloud those series usually come from a Prometheus-compatible data source (often Grafana-hosted metrics storage, not a Prometheus server you run yourself). Synthetic checks and many application metrics show up there, so you can graph probe latency for the same wall-clock span as your k6 run. This milestone starts with metrics because they give a quick, regional view of health before you dig into logs and traces. Refer to [Explore](/docs/grafana/latest/explore/) if you need a refresher on the page.

You finished the spike and k6 review milestones in Grafana Cloud k6 with a UTC window and summary numbers. This milestone focuses on **synthetic probe** latency in your Prometheus-compatible data source for that same window (separate from the `grafanacloud-k6` generator metrics you aligned earlier).

k6 and synthetic monitoring solve different problems day to day. Here you only borrow probe time series as a second camera on the spike: probes keep their normal schedule, and Prometheus stores their samples during your test window. That lets you compare what outside regions saw against what the load generator reported, without treating runtime checks as a substitute for a performance test.

Synthetic probes measure the endpoint from multiple regions. They often diverge from k6's numbers because path, DNS, TLS, geography, and concurrency differ. Your job is to see whether user-visible latency spiked in the same window as the test.

To analyze probe metrics, complete the following steps.

1. In the main Grafana navigation on the left, open **Explore**.

   If the navigation is collapsed, open it with the menu control, then choose **Explore**.

1. Select your Prometheus-compatible data source.

   Demo stacks sometimes use a name like `grafanacloud-demoinfra-prom`; yours may differ.

1. Set the time range to cover your test window from the previous milestone.

   Include warm-up and cool-down.

1. Run the following query and switch to a **Time series** visualization.

```promql
probe_http_duration_seconds
```

   If the metric is absent, ask your administrator which metric exposes synthetic HTTP latency for the quickpizza checks and substitute it while keeping the same questions.

1. Fill the probe chart table from `probe_http_duration_seconds`.

| Question | Your value |
| --- | --- |
| Latency spike in the test window? | |
| Region that peaked highest | |
| Region slowest to recover | |
| Worst probe peak vs k6 p95 (rough multiple) | |

1. Run the following query (or the closest probe-phase metric your environment exposes).

```promql
probe_duration_seconds
```

1. Fill the recovery table from that chart.

| Question | Your value |
| --- | --- |
| Returned to baseline by end of range? | |
| Minutes of degraded experience (slowest region) | |

| Reflection | Your answer (1–2 sentences) |
| --- | --- |
| Why might probes report higher latency than k6 for the same URL? | |

Check your work: you can move on when the **Explore** time range matches the k6 window, both queries return data (or you documented equivalent metric names), both tables are filled, and you answered the reflection row. If panels are empty, widen the range a few minutes, confirm the data source, then open the troubleshooting topics for this milestone.

In the next milestone you validate whether errors accompanied the latency change.
